title: Stunting vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: [Stunting](#dod:stunting) measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Stunting vs. GDP per capita

Stunting measures the share of children younger than 5 years that are too short for their age. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of children under five who are stunted and GDP per capita for various countries in 2024, with GDP per capita expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale ranging from roughly $1,000 to over $100,000, while the vertical axis measures the stunting rate from 0% to over 50%. A clear negative correlation is visible: countries with lower GDP per capita generally exhibit higher stunting rates. In the lower-left quadrant, nations such as Burundi, Niger, and the Democratic Republic of Congo cluster with GDP per capita below $2,000 and stunting rates between 40% and 55%. Moving toward the center, countries like India, Pakistan, and Guatemala appear with GDP per capita between $5,000 and $10,000 and stunting rates ranging from approximately 30% to 45%. As GDP per capita increases beyond $20,000, the data points shift significantly downward. Nations including Argentina, Panama, and Malaysia show stunting rates between 10% and 25%. At the far right of the chart, countries with GDP per capita exceeding $50,000, such as Brunei, Estonia, and Australia, consistently display stunting rates below 10%, with several points hovering near the 0% baseline. The legend categorizes these observations by region, showing that African nations (purple dots) are predominantly located in the high-stunting, low-income section, while European (blue) and North American (orange) nations are concentrated in the low-stunting, high-income section.
